children.name: Tyrannus
children.description: The kingbirds — bold, assertive flycatchers of open country across the Americas. They are among the largest tyrant flycatchers, with strong bills and a habit of sallying from exposed perches to catch insects on the wing. Several species perform spectacular aerial displays and are noted for aggressive defence of their territories against much larger birds including hawks and crows.

children.name: Tyrannus tyrannus
children.description: A common and conspicuous flycatcher of open country across North America. Adults are dark grey above, white below, with a white terminal tail band that is diagnostic in flight. Eastern Kingbirds are famously aggressive in defence of their nests, mobbing hawks, crows, and even humans that approach too closely. They winter in the Amazon basin, travelling up to 8,000 km each year.

children.name: Tyrannus forficatus
children.description: One of the most elegant North American birds, with an extraordinarily long, forked tail that can exceed the body length. The salmon-pink flanks and underwing linings flash in flight during graceful aerial manoeuvres. It breeds in grasslands and savannas of the south-central United States and winters in Central America. The tail of the male reaches full length after the second year.

children.name: Tyrannus savana
children.description: A spectacular flycatcher with an extremely long, deeply forked tail that trails behind it like a ribbon as it flies through open grasslands and savannas. It ranges from southern Mexico to central Argentina, with southern populations undertaking long-distance migrations. The male's tail can reach up to 40 cm, longer than its entire body.

children.name: Tyrannus melancholicus
children.description: The most widespread kingbird, occurring from the southwestern United States through Central and South America to central Argentina. It is a large, pale-bellied kingbird with a notched tail and a distinctive, twittering call. A highly adaptable species found in almost any open or semi-open habitat, it is often seen perched on fences and utility wires.

children.name: Tyrannus verticalis
children.description: A pale grey-and-yellow kingbird of open western North America, distinguished by its black tail with white outer edges. It is a conspicuous bird of farmland, grassland, and desert scrub, often seen sallying from fences and utility wires. Its high, sputtering call and energetic aerial pursuits make it one of the most visible flycatchers in the American West.

children.name: Tyrannus vociferans
children.description: A large, dark-headed kingbird with a distinctive white malar stripe and a deeply forked tail edged with pale rufous. It breeds in open highlands from the southwestern United States through Mexico, wintering south to Central America. Its loud, harsh 'chi-beer' call and aggressive territorial behaviour distinguish it from the similar Western Kingbird.

children.name: Myiarchus
children.description: The Myiarchus flycatchers — a widespread genus of medium to large tyrant flycatchers found from North America through South America. They are characterised by rufous-edged wings and tail, a bushy crest, and a pale yellow belly. Most species are difficult to distinguish visually and are best identified by their distinctive calls and habitat preferences.

children.name: Myiarchus tyrannulus
children.description: The largest Myiarchus in North America, with a heavy bill and a loud, rolling 'whee-eep' call. Its brown upperparts and pale yellow belly are typical of the genus, but its thick black bill and extensive rufous on the inner webs of the tail feathers are key identifiers. It inhabits arid and semi-arid woodlands from the southwestern United States through Central America to Argentina.

children.name: Myiarchus crinitus
children.description: The only Myiarchus that breeds in eastern North America, recognised by its loud, whistled 'wheep' call and warm brown upperparts with a bright lemon-yellow belly. It is a cavity nester that readily uses nest boxes, often incorporating a shed snakeskin into its nest — a curious behaviour whose function remains unknown. It winters in southern Florida, Mexico, and the Caribbean.

children.name: Myiarchus cinerascens
children.description: A pale, subtly coloured Myiarchus of arid western North America, with a grey throat, pale yellow belly, and rufous-fringed wings. It is smaller and paler than the Brown-crested Flycatcher, with which it coexists in some areas. It breeds in desert scrub, chaparral, and dry woodland, nesting in natural cavities and old woodpecker holes.

children.name: Myiarchus tuberculifer
children.description: A small, dark Myiarchus distinguished by its dusky crown and nape, relatively short bill, and plaintive, descending whistle. It ranges from southern Arizona to northern Argentina, inhabiting humid forest and woodland edges across a wide elevational range. Its simple, sorrowful call is a characteristic sound of Neotropical forests.

children.name: Myiarchus nuttingi
children.description: A medium-sized Myiarchus of dry tropical forests from Mexico to Costa Rica, very similar to the Ash-throated Flycatcher but with a paler throat and a slightly smaller bill. Its call is a clear, whistled 'prreeet' that helps distinguish it from other members of the genus. It inhabits arid to semi-arid woodland, riparian corridors, and scrubby forest edges.

children.name: Myiarchus cephalotes
children.description: A montane Myiarchus found in the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia at elevations of 1,500 to 3,000 metres. It has olive-brown upperparts, pale yellow underparts, and distinctive pale edges on the wing coverts. It inhabits humid montane forest and cloud forest edges, often foraging in the mid-story for insects and small fruits.

children.name: Myiarchus phaeocephalus
children.description: A dark-crowned Myiarchus found in arid lowlands of western Ecuador and northwestern Peru. Its sooty-grey crown contrasts with a paler face and throat, and it has a relatively short bill for the genus. It inhabits dry forest, scrub, and mangroves, often perching conspicuously on exposed branches and snapping at passing insects.

children.name: Myiarchus magnirostris
children.description: The only Myiarchus species endemic to the Galapagos Islands, found on most of the major islands. It is a relatively small Myiarchus with a notably large, broad bill adapted for the archipelago's unique food resources. It is a fearless and curious bird, often approaching humans closely in the arid lowlands and transition zones of the islands.

children.name: Pitangus
children.description: The kiskadees — large, stocky flycatchers with a notably heavy bill and a bold black-and-white head pattern. They are among the most versatile foragers in the family, taking insects, lizards, small fish, and fruit, and often snatching prey from the water's surface like a kingfisher. Their loud, three-syllable call gives them their onomatopoeic common name.

children.name: Pitangus sulphuratus
children.description: A large, unmistakable flycatcher with a brilliant yellow belly, rufous wings, and a striking black-and-white striped head. It ranges from southern Texas to central Argentina and is one of the most familiar birds in the Neotropics. Unusually for a tyrant flycatcher, it frequently takes fruit, fish, and even nestlings of other birds, demonstrating remarkable dietary flexibility.

children.name: Pitangus lictor
children.description: A smaller, more slender relative of the Great Kiskadee, found in lowland wetlands from Panama to the Amazon. It has the same bold black-and-white head pattern but is noticeably smaller with a less massive bill and more olive upperparts. It is closely associated with water, perching on emergent vegetation and snatching insects from the surface.

children.name: Megarynchus
children.description: The boat-billed flycatcher — a monotypic genus whose most distinctive feature is an extraordinarily broad, deep bill that resembles an inverted boat hull. It is closely related to the kiskadees but has a larger bill and more deliberate foraging style. Found from Mexico to Argentina, it inhabits forest edges and open woodlands.

children.name: Megarynchus pitangua
children.description: A large flycatcher with an enormous, broad bill that is remarkably flat and wide, resembling the hull of a boat. It is similar in plumage to the Great Kiskadee but even larger of bill and lacking the rufous wings. It forages with a deliberate, patient style, scanning for large insects, lizards, and fruit from exposed perches in the canopy.

children.name: Pyrocephalus
children.description: Vermilion flycatchers — small, striking flycatchers in which the male is one of the most brilliantly coloured of all passerines. The male is vivid scarlet-orange on the crown, underparts, and rump, contrasting with dark brown upperparts. They are birds of open habitats near water, where they sally for insects from low, exposed perches in a characteristic upright posture.

children.name: Pyrocephalus rubinus
children.description: A dazzling small flycatcher in which the male is a brilliant vermilion-red on the crown, breast, and underparts, with dark brown upperparts and a dark mask through the eye. Females are a subtle greyish-brown with a salmon-pink wash on the belly. It inhabits open woodland, savanna, and riparian areas from the southwestern United States through the tropics to central Argentina.

children.name: Fluvicola
children.description: The water tyrants — small, black-and-white flycatchers strongly associated with aquatic habitats. They are among the most specialised tyrant flycatchers for riparian and wetland environments, often seen flitting low over water or perching on emergent vegetation. Their pied plumage and active, bouncy flight make them conspicuous residents of marshes and river edges.

children.name: Fluvicola pica
children.description: A sprightly black-and-white flycatcher of lowland wetlands from Panama to the Amazon. It has a white head and underparts with a black back, wings, and tail, giving it a striking magpie-like appearance. It forages low over water, snapping at insects on the surface, and builds a distinctive ball-shaped nest with a side entrance in marsh vegetation.

children.name: Sayornis
children.description: The phoebes — medium-sized flycatchers named for their vocalised 'fee-bee' song, which they deliver from an exposed perch. They are more tail-pumping than other flycatchers, habitually dipping their tail when perched. Phoebes are among the first flycatchers to return to northern breeding grounds in spring and build mud-and-grass nests on ledges, bridges, and buildings.

children.name: Sayornis phoebe
children.description: A familiar flycatcher of eastern North America, recognised by its emphatic 'fee-bee' song and constant tail-wagging. It was one of the first bird species in North America to be banded by John James Audubon, who tied silver threads to its legs in 1804 to track returns. It readily nests on buildings and bridge ledges, often returning to the same site year after year.

children.name: Sayornis saya
children.description: A pale, buff-and-grey phoebe of western North America's arid and semi-arid regions. It is more likely to be found in dry canyons, sagebrush, and desert scrub than its eastern cousin. Its song is a soft, plaintive 'pit-see-ar' reminiscent of a robin's call. Named after the American naturalist Thomas Say, it is an early spring arrival on its breeding grounds.

children.name: Sayornis nigricans
children.description: A striking black-and-white phoebe found near water from the southwestern United States to Argentina. Its dark sooty-black body contrasts with a clean white belly and a constant bobbing tail motion. It builds a mud nest plastered to vertical surfaces under bridges, culverts, and building eaves, almost always within reach of water.

children.name: Empidonax
children.description: The Empidonax flycatchers — a famously challenging genus of small, near-identical flycatchers best distinguished by voice, habitat, and subtle plumage differences. They are among the most difficult birds to identify in the field, with species often separated primarily by their distinctive songs and breeding habitats. Despite their plain appearance, they are agile aerial insectivores with a brisk, purposeful manner.

children.name: Empidonax traillii
children.description: A small, olive-brown flycatcher with a pale yellowish belly and two whitish wing bars. It is best identified by its aggressive 'fitz-bew' song, which differs from the similar 'che-bek' of the Alder Flycatcher. It breeds in dense willow thickets and other shrubby wetlands across North America and winters in Central America. The Southwestern subspecies E. t. extimus is listed as endangered under the US Endangered Species Act.

children.name: Empidonax minimus
children.description: The smallest Empidonax in North America, barely 13 cm in length, with a prominent white eye-ring and short primary projection. Its explosive 'che-bek' song is one of the most distinctive sounds of northern deciduous woodlands in spring and summer. It has a strong, repeated 'che-bek' call that carries well through the canopy, making it one of the easier Empidonax to locate and identify by ear.

children.name: Empidonax virescens
children.description: A greenish-tinged Empidonax of mature deciduous forests in eastern North America, with a bold white eye-ring and long, broad bill. Its explosive 'peet-sa!' song, delivered from the understory, sounds like a sudden whip crack. It requires large, intact forest tracts and is sensitive to fragmentation, wintering in the foothills of the Andes from Colombia to Peru.

children.name: Empidonax alnorum
children.description: A boreal specialist that breeds in alder and willow thickets across Canada and Alaska. It is essentially indistinguishable from the Willow Flycatcher in appearance, but its burry 'ree-BEE-o' song is diagnostic. It migrates to the Amazon basin, one of the longest migrations of any Empidonax.

children.name: Empidonax hammondii
children.description: A small, greyish Empidonax of montane coniferous forests in western North America. It has a short tail, a peaked crown, and a distinctive 'chu-peek' song. It forages in the upper canopy, sallying outward from exposed perch tips, a behaviour that distinguishes it from the similar Dusky Flycatcher.

children.name: Empidonax oberholseri
children.description: A plain, olive-grey Empidonax of brushy mountain slopes and chaparral in western North America. It is best identified by its song — a soft, two-part 'swee-dit' — and its habit of pumping its tail downward while perched. It winters in western Mexico and prefers drier, more open habitats than the closely related Hammond's Flycatcher.

children.name: Empidonax occidentalis
children.description: A yellow-bellied Empidonax of highland forests and canyons in the Rocky Mountains and Sierra Madre Occidental. Its eye-ring is teardrop-shaped and its call is a clear, rising 'seet-seet'. It was formerly considered conspecific with the Pacific-slope Flycatcher under the name 'Western Flycatcher' before being split.

children.name: Empidonax difficilis
children.description: A yellow-bellied Empidonax of humid coastal forests from Alaska to Mexico. It has a distinctive teardrop-shaped eye-ring, a flattened head, and a soft, ascending 'pseet' call. It nests in mossy crevices on stream banks, road cuts, and the roots of fallen trees in the damp understory of Pacific coniferous forests.

children.name: Empidonax flaviventris
children.description: The yellowest Empidonax, with a bright yellow throat, belly, and wing bars that distinguish it from all other species in the genus. It breeds in the boreal bogs and coniferous swamps of Canada and the northern United States, wintering in lowland forests from Mexico to Panama. Its song is a soft, burry 'che-lek'.

children.name: Empidonax fulvifrons
children.description: A small, warm-toned Empidonax with a distinctive buffy wash across the breast, a prominent white eye-ring, and two pale wing bars. It ranges from the southwestern United States through Mexico to Honduras, inhabiting pine-oak woodlands and dry montane forests. It is the only Empidonax with a buffy breast, making it one of the easier species to identify by sight.

children.name: Empidonax affinis
children.description: A highland Empidonax of pine and pine-oak forests from northwestern Mexico to Honduras. It is very similar to the Cordilleran Flycatcher but has a duller yellow belly and a slightly different song. It is a bird of the mid-story and canopy, sallying for insects from exposed perches in the upper branches of tall pines.

children.name: Empidonax albigularis
children.description: A dark Empidonax of wet lowlands and marshes from southeastern Mexico to Panama. It has a distinctive white throat that contrasts with its olive-brown breast and a relatively long tail. It inhabits dense thickets, marsh edges, and humid forest understory, where its sharp, metallic call notes reveal its presence.

children.name: Contopus
children.description: The pewees and wood-pewees — slender, long-winged flycatchers that sit erect on exposed dead branches, sallying out after passing insects. They are named for the plaintive, whistled 'pee-a-wee' song of the Eastern Wood-Pewee. Contopus species are among the last flycatchers to arrive in spring and among the first to depart in autumn, undertaking long migrations to tropical wintering grounds.

children.name: Contopus cooperi
children.description: A large, robust pewee with a distinctive vested appearance: dark olive sides contrast sharply with a white throat and belly. It breeds in boreal and montane coniferous forests across North America and winters in the Andes. Its emphatic 'quick-THREE-BEERS' song carries from the highest exposed perches. It has declined significantly due to habitat loss on both breeding and wintering grounds.

children.name: Contopus virens
children.description: A small, greyish-olive pewee of eastern North American deciduous and mixed forests. It sings a plaintive, whistled 'pee-a-wee' throughout the day, even at midday heat when most other songbirds fall silent. It builds a an lichen-covered cup nest on horizontal branches and winters in the foothills of the northern Andes.

children.name: Contopus sordidulus
children.description: A dusky-olive pewee of western North America, nearly identical to the Eastern Wood Pewee but with a darker throat and a buzzy, descending 'peeer' song. It breeds in coniferous and mixed woodlands from Alaska to Mexico, often choosing the highest available dead snag as its singing perch. It winters in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia.

children.name: Contopus cinereus
children.description: A small, warm brown pewee of open woodlands and forest edges from southern Mexico to Argentina. It lacks the strong wing bars of its northern relatives and has a softer, more melodic song. It is most often detected by its repeated, rising whistled notes delivered from a low exposed perch in the forest understory.

children.name: Contopus pertinax
children.description: A large, crested pewee of pine-oak and highland forests from the southwestern United States through Mexico to Honduras. It is the largest Contopus in North America and has a distinctive trilling song that descends in pitch. Its upright posture and prominent crest give it a more regal appearance than other pewees.

children.name: Contopus lugubris
children.description: A sombre, dark grey pewee endemic to the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama. It lacks the wing bars typical of most Contopus species and has a dusky breast and belly. Its song is a mournful, descending whistle, and it inhabits montane cloud forests and oak woodlands at elevations of 1,500 to 3,000 metres.

children.name: Contopus nigrescens
children.description: A very dark, almost sooty-black pewee found in lowland and foothill forests of the Amazon basin from eastern Ecuador to Bolivia and Brazil. It is one of the darkest Contopus species, with faint wing bars and a slender silhouette. It perches in the mid-story and subcanopy of humid forests, sallying for insects in the dim understory.

children.name: Contopus fumigatus
children.description: A smoky-grey pewee of Andean cloud forests from Venezuela to Bolivia at elevations of 1,000 to 2,800 metres. Its plumage is uniformly greyish-brown with a slightly paler belly and faint wing bars. It is a common but inconspicuous bird of forest edges and clearings, where its soft, trilling song is often heard before the bird is seen.

children.name: Myiozetetes
children.description: The social flycatchers — medium-sized tyrant flycatchers that live up to their name, often found in noisy, active groups. They resemble small kiskadees with a similar black-and-white head pattern but are more slender and gregarious. They are common in semi-open habitats from Mexico to Argentina, often around human habitation.

children.name: Myiozetetes similis
children.description: A common and conspicuous flycatcher from Mexico to northwestern Argentina, easily confused with the Great Kiskadee but smaller with a less massive bill. True to its name, it is highly social, often found in small groups that call constantly and chase each other through the trees. It builds a large domed nest with a side entrance, frequently placed near wasp nests for protection.

children.name: Myiozetetes granadensis
children.description: A close relative of the Social Flycatcher, distinguished by a grey crown (not black) and a more southerly range from Honduras to the Amazon. It has the same kiskadee-like head pattern but the grey cap gives it a softer, less contrasted appearance. It prefers humid forest edges and clearings, often near water.

children.name: Myiozetetes luteiventris
children.description: A distinctive Myiozetetes with a dark olive breast and a bright yellow belly, lacking the bold black-and-white head pattern of its congeners. It inhabits lowland rainforest in the western Amazon basin, where it forages in the mid-story for insects and fruit. Its vocalisations are higher pitched and less harsh than those of the Social Flycatcher.

children.name: Machetornis
children.description: The cattle tyrant — a unique terrestrial flycatcher that has abandoned the typical aerial sallying behaviour of the family. It is the only tyrant flycatcher to have adapted to a largely ground-dwelling lifestyle, running on long legs after insects disturbed by grazing livestock. Its robust tarsi and reduced wing length reflect this terrestrial shift.

children.name: Machetornis rixosa
children.description: A remarkable ground-dwelling flycatcher that forages at the feet of cattle and other large mammals, catching insects flushed by their movement. It has unusually long legs and a reduced wing compared with other tyrant flycatchers, adapting it to a terrestrial lifestyle. It often perches on the backs of livestock and will hitch rides on their heads, scanning for prey.

children.name: Attila
children.description: The attilas — stocky, hook-billed tyrant flycatchers of Neotropical lowland forests. Despite their drab plumage in shades of rufous, olive, and yellow, they have powerful, melodious songs that carry through the forest. Their hooked bill tips reveal a more predatory nature than most tyrant flycatchers, and they are known to take small vertebrates as well as large insects and fruit.

children.name: Attila spadiceus
children.description: The most widespread Attila, found from northwestern Mexico through Central America to the Amazon and Bolivia. It has olive upperparts, a bright yellow rump, and a pale yellow belly with a rufous tail. Its song is a loud, far-carrying, musical series of whistled notes that is one of the characteristic sounds of Neotropical lowland forests.

children.name: Attila torridus
children.description: A striking rufous-and-yellow Attila found in the lowlands of western Ecuador and adjacent Peru. It has a warm ochraceous head and breast, bright yellow belly, and a dark tail. It inhabits humid lowland and foothill forests, where its loud, whistled song is often heard from the mid-story and canopy.

children.name: Attila rufus
children.description: An Attila endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, with a distinctive grey hood, olive back, and bright yellow belly. It is a bird of humid lowland and montane forests, often seen in pairs or small groups in the mid-story. Its song is a loud, clear series of whistled notes that echoes through the forest.

children.name: Legatus
children.description: The piratic flycatcher — a monotypic genus of medium-sized tyrant flycatchers whose name derives from its aggressive behaviour of usurping nests built by other birds. It does not build its own nest but instead evicts the owners of domed nests and raises its own young in them. It ranges from Mexico to Argentina in lowland forests and clearings.

children.name: Legatus leucophaius
children.description: A small but feisty flycatcher named for its piratical nesting behaviour: it commandeers the domed nests of other birds, particularly oropendolas, caciques, and becards. It has brown upperparts, a whitish throat, and streaked underparts with a hint of yellow. It is a common bird of forest edges, clearings, and gardens from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Tyrannopsis
children.description: The sulphury flycatcher — a monotypic genus of large, striking flycatchers found in the Amazon and Guianan regions. It resembles a large kiskadee but with a distinctively short, heavy bill and a more greenish back. It is a bird of palm savannas, forest edges, and riverine woodlands, often seen perched prominently on palm fronds.

children.name: Tyrannopsis sulphurea
children.description: A large, handsome flycatcher with bright yellow underparts, a greenish-olive back, and a dark cap with a concealed yellow crown patch. It is strongly associated with palm trees, particularly moriche palms, in the Amazon and Guianan lowlands. Its diet consists of large insects and fruit, and it often nests in palm fronds.

children.name: Empidonomus
children.description: The variegated flycatcher — a monotypic genus of medium-sized tyrant flycatchers found in South America. Its plumage is an attractive combination of dark upperparts with bold white wing bars and pale underparts with subtle streaking. It is a bird of open woodlands and forest edges, often seen sallying for insects from exposed perches.

children.name: Empidonomus varius
children.description: A handsome, medium-sized flycatcher with dark brown upperparts streaked with buff, bold whitish wing bars, and pale yellowish underparts with dark streaking on the breast. It ranges across much of South America east of the Andes, from Colombia to northern Argentina. It is a migratory species, with southern populations moving northward during the austral winter.

children.name: Colonia
children.description: The long-tailed tyrant — a monotypic genus of elegant, slender flycatchers with an extraordinarily long, white-tipped tail. The male's tail is deeply forked and can exceed the body length, streaming behind it as it flies through the forest understory. Found from Honduras to the Amazon, it inhabits humid lowland forests and clearings.

children.name: Colonia colonus
children.description: A striking flycatcher with a glossy black cap, white face, and an exceptionally long, white-tipped black tail that streams behind it in flight. It ranges from Honduras through Central America to the Amazon basin and Brazil. It perches conspicuously on exposed branches and snags in the forest canopy, sallying out to catch insects on the wing.

children.name: Knipolegus
children.description: The black tyrants — a genus of tyrant flycatchers found primarily in South America, characterised by strong sexual dimorphism. Males are predominantly dark grey or black with striking white or red wing patches; females are usually brown with buffy markings. They inhabit open and semi-open habitats from lowland savannas to high Andean grasslands.

children.name: Knipolegus cyanirostris
children.description: A small black tyrant found in the Atlantic Forest and adjacent regions of southeastern Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. The male is entirely glossy black with a striking pale blue-grey bill; the female is brown with pale streaking. It inhabits forest edges, clearings, and secondary growth, perching low and sallying for insects.
